---
title: "Appendix B: Probability & Statistics for PCRA"
subtitle: "Distributions, Estimation, Least Squares, and Asymptotic Theory"
---

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
**Copyright Notice.** Planned for publication in 2026 by R. Douglas Martin, Thomas K. Philips, Bernd Scherer, and Kirk Li. All rights reserved. © Copyright 2025.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
&#128196; **Full appendix available as PDF.** [Download Appendix B — Probability & Statistics for PCRA](Appendix%20B%20ProbStat%20for%20PCRA.pdf){target="_blank"}
:::

## Overview

This appendix contains a variety of probability and statistics results that are useful in the context of portfolio construction and risk management theory and methods.

---

## B.1 — Location and Scale Distributions

Let $R$ be a random variable with location parameter $\mu$ and scale parameter $s>0$. The probability distribution function $F_{R}(r)$ and density function $f_{R}(r)$ have the forms

$$
F_{R}(r) = F\!\left(\frac{r-\mu}{s}\right), \qquad f_{R}(r) = \frac{1}{s}f\!\left(\frac{r-\mu}{s}\right).
$$

Roughly speaking, the location parameter tells us where $f_{R}(r)$ is centered, while the scale parameter tells us how widely it spreads.

The most famous member of the location-scale family is the **Normal distribution**:

$$
f_{R}(r)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right), \quad -\infty<r<\infty.
$$

The location parameter $\mu$ is the mean and the scale parameter $\sigma$ is the standard deviation.

A closely related family is the **Student's $t$-distribution**:

$$
f_{R}(r)=\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)\sqrt{\pi\nu\tau^{2}}}\left(1+\frac{1}{\nu}\frac{(r-\mu)^{2}}{\tau^{2}}\right)^{-\frac{\nu+1}{2}}, \quad -\infty<r<\infty
$$

with location parameter $\mu$ and scale parameter $\tau$. For $\nu>1$ the mean is $\mu$, and for $\nu>2$ the variance is $\tau^{2}\cdot\tfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}$. When $\nu=1$ the distribution is a **Cauchy distribution** whose mean is undefined. Heavy-tailed distributions such as the $t$ motivate the robust estimation methods developed throughout PCRA.

A less well-known but important member of the location-scale family is the **Laplacian** (two-sided exponential) distribution:

$$
f_{R}(r)=\frac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\frac{|r-\mu|}{b}\right), \quad -\infty<r<\infty.
$$

The location parameter $\mu$ is the mean, and the scale parameter $b$ is $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ times its standard deviation.

---

## B.2 — Invariance Properties of Location, Scale, Covariance and Correlation

### Mean and Standard Deviation Equivariance

Let $\mu_{R}=\mathrm{E}[R]$ and $\sigma_{R}=\sqrt{\mathrm{var}(R)}$, and let $\dot{R}=a+bR$ be an **affine transformation** of $R$ with $b>0$. Then

$$
\mu_{\dot{R}} = a+b\mu_{R}, \qquad \sigma_{\dot{R}} = b\cdot\sigma_{R}.
$$

The mean is **location and scale equivariant**; the standard deviation is **location invariant and scale equivariant**.

### Covariance Translation Invariance and Scale Equivariance

Suppose $\dot{r}_{i}=a+b\cdot r_{i}$ and $\dot{r}_{j}=c+d\cdot r_{j}$. Then

$$
\mathrm{cov}(\dot{r}_{i},\,\dot{r}_{j})=b\cdot d\cdot\mathrm{cov}(r_{i},\,r_{j}).
$$

Covariances are **translation invariant** and **scale equivariant**.

### Correlation Affine Invariance

Since $\mathrm{cor}(r_{i},r_{j})=\mathrm{cov}(r_{i},r_{j})/(\sigma_{r_{i}}\sigma_{r_{j}})$, it follows that

$$
\mathrm{cor}(\dot{r}_{i},\,\dot{r}_{j})=\mathrm{cor}(r_{i},\,r_{j}).
$$

**Correlations are affine invariant.** The above equivariance and invariance properties also hold for the corresponding sample-based estimators.

---

## B.3 — Linearity of Covariances

Suppose $z=\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_{i}y_{i}$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{cov}(x,\,z) &= \mathrm{cov}\!\left(x,\,\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_{i}y_{i}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}a_{i}\,\mathrm{cov}(x,\,y_{i}) = \mathbf{a}^{\prime}\mathrm{cov}(x,\,\mathbf{y})
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathrm{cov}(x,\mathbf{y})$ is an $n$-dimensional column vector with $i^{th}$ element $\mathrm{cov}(x,y_{i})$.

A leading application is the covariance between the return of a portfolio $r_{P}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}w_{i}r_{i}=\mathbf{w}^{\prime}\mathbf{r}$ and its $i^{th}$ constituent asset:

$$
\mathrm{cov}(r_{i},\,r_{P})=\sum_{j=1}^{n}w_{j}\,\mathrm{cov}(r_{i},\,r_{j})=\mathbf{w}^{\prime}\mathrm{cov}(r_{i},\,\mathbf{r}).
$$

---

## B.4 — Estimator Bias, Variance and Mean Squared Error

### Unbiased versus Biased Estimators

An **unbiased** estimator $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ satisfies $\mathrm{E}[\hat{\theta}_{n}]=\theta$ for all $\theta\in\Theta$. When this fails, the estimator is **biased** with bias

$$
B(\hat{\theta}_{n})=\mathrm{E}[\hat{\theta}_{n}]-\theta.
$$

The sample mean $\bar{r}=\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}r_{t}$ is unbiased for $\mu$. The sample variance with divisor $n$,

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{n}^{2}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(r_{t}-\bar{r})^{2},
$$

has a negative bias $B(\hat{\sigma}_{n}^{2})=-\tfrac{1}{n}\sigma^{2}$, while the unbiased version uses divisor $n-1$:

$$
S_{n}^{2}=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(r_{t}-\bar{r})^{2}, \qquad \mathrm{E}[S_{n}^{2}]=\sigma^{2}.
$$

For normally distributed returns, Holtzman (1950) and Ahn and Fessler (2003) show that an almost perfectly unbiased estimate of $\sigma$ for $n\geqslant4$ is

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{n}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1.5}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(r_{t}-\bar{r})^{2}}.
$$

If exact unbiasedness is not achievable for every finite $n$, one should use a **consistent** estimator whose bias goes to zero as $n\to\infty$.

### Estimator Mean Squared Error

The **mean squared error** (MSE) of $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ is

$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat{\theta}_{n})=\mathrm{E}\!\left[(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta)^{2}\right]=\mathrm{var}(\hat{\theta}_{n})+B^{2}(\hat{\theta}_{n}).
$$

For a vector parameter $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Theta}}_{n}$:

$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Theta}}_{n})=\mathrm{E}\!\left[\|\hat{\boldsymbol{\Theta}}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\Theta}\|^{2}\right]=\sum_{i}\mathrm{var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Theta}}_{i,n})+B^{2}(\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{i}).
$$

When estimating a vector of parameters, it is sometimes possible to admit a small bias in exchange for a much lower variance (Stein 1956), so that the Minimum MSE estimator is slightly biased.

---

## B.5 — Least Squares

### Ordinary Least Squares

Given observations $y_{i},\ x_{i1},\ldots,x_{im}$, $i=1,\ldots,n$, the **standard linear regression model** is

$$
y_{i}=\beta_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{m}\beta_{j}x_{ij}+\epsilon_{i}.
$$

In matrix-vector notation with $\mathbf{X}=[\mathbf{1}_{n},\mathbf{x}_{1},\ldots,\mathbf{x}_{m}]$:

$$
\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}+\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}.
$$

The **Ordinary Least Squares** (OLS) estimates minimize the sum of squared errors:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}=\underset{\boldsymbol{\beta}}{\mathrm{argmin}}\,(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^{\prime}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}).
$$

Setting the gradient to zero gives the **normal equations** $\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}=\mathbf{0}$, and if $\mathbf{X}$ has full rank:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}=(\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{y}.
$$

The OLS fit $\hat{\mathbf{y}}=\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}=\mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$ uses the **Hat matrix** (or Projection matrix)

$$
\mathbf{H}=\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\prime},
$$

which is symmetric and idempotent ($\mathbf{H}^{\prime}=\mathbf{H}$, $\mathbf{H}^{2}=\mathbf{H}$). The residuals are

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}=(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}
$$

where $\mathbf{M}=\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}$ is the **Annihilator matrix**. The OLS fit is a projection — it projects $\mathbf{y}$ onto the column space of $\mathbf{X}$, so residuals are orthogonal to all columns of $\mathbf{X}$.

::: {#fig-ols layout-ncol=1}
![OLS Orthogonal Projection and the Fit-plus-Residuals Decomposition. The OLS fit $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ lies in the column space of $\mathbf{X}$, and the residuals $\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}$ are orthogonal to it. Points far from the line (large residuals) have an outsized influence on the OLS estimate — a key motivation for robust regression.](0C__Users_tkpme_Dropbox_BookPCRA_Springer_Produ_____Stat_for_PCRM_Plots_OLS_Fit_Plus_Residual.png){fig-alt="OLS fit with residual plot"}

OLS Orthogonal Projection and the Fit-plus-Residuals Decomposition.
:::

Even though OLS has an elegant theoretical foundation, it is **not robust to outliers**: the OLS coefficients are linear in $\mathbf{y}$, so even a single outlier can make the bias in $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}$ arbitrarily large.

### Weighted Least Squares

Let $\mathbf{W}$ be a diagonal weight matrix with $\mathbf{W}_{ii}\geqslant0$. The **Weighted Least Squares** (WLS) fit minimizes

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{WLS}=\underset{\boldsymbol{\beta}}{\mathrm{argmin}}\,(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^{\prime}\mathbf{W}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}),
$$

with solution (when $\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}$ is invertible):

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{WLS}=(\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{y}.
$$

WLS is equivalent to OLS applied to scaled response and predictor variables $(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{y},\,\mathbf{V}\mathbf{X})$ where $\mathbf{V}^{\prime}\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{W}$. If errors are heteroskedastic with known variances $\sigma_{i}^{2}$, the natural choice is $w_{ii}=1/\sigma_{i}^{2}$, yielding the MLE under normality. Robust regression methods are at heart specialized WLS variants that automate the identification of outliers using data-dependent weights

$$
w_{ii}=\frac{\psi(\hat{\epsilon}_{i})}{\hat{\epsilon}_{i}},
$$

where $\psi(\cdot)$ is a nonlinear transformation that systematically underweights outliers.

### Generalized Least Squares

**Generalized Least Squares** (GLS) handles correlated errors with known covariance matrix $\mathbf{C}$:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{GLS}=\underset{\boldsymbol{\beta}}{\mathrm{argmin}}\,(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^{\prime}\mathbf{C}^{-1}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}).
$$

When $\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{C}^{-1}\mathbf{X}$ is invertible:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{GLS}=(\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{C}^{-1}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{C}^{-1}\mathbf{y}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{C}^{-1}=\boldsymbol{\Omega}^{\prime}\boldsymbol{\Omega}$, GLS is equivalent to OLS applied to $(\boldsymbol{\Omega}\mathbf{y},\,\boldsymbol{\Omega}\mathbf{X})$, simultaneously scaling and decorrelating the errors. Like OLS, GLS is not robust to outliers. Furthermore, $\mathbf{C}$ is usually unknown and must be estimated from data, which erodes much of its theoretical advantage.

### Least Squares Inference with Normally Distributed Errors

If $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}|\mathbf{X}\sim\mathrm{MVN}(0,\sigma^{2}\mathbf{I}_{n})$, then

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}-\boldsymbol{\beta}=(\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\sim\mathrm{MVN}(0,\,\sigma^{2}(\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{X})^{-1}).
$$

An unbiased estimate of $\sigma^{2}$ is $s^{2}=\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}^{\prime}\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}/(n-m-1)$, and the standard error of the $j^{th}$ coefficient is

$$
SE(\hat{\beta}_{j,OLS})=\sqrt{s^{2}\times((\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{X})^{-1})_{j,j}}.
$$

To test $H_{0}:\beta_{j}=\overline{\beta_{j}}$, the **t-statistic**

$$
t_{j}=\frac{\hat{\beta}_{j,OLS}-\overline{\beta_{j}}}{SE(\hat{\beta}_{j,OLS})}
$$

has a $t$ distribution with $n-m-1$ degrees of freedom; reject $H_0$ if $|t_{j}|>t_{\alpha/2}(n-m-1)$.

For the general linear hypothesis $H_{0}:\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}=\mathbf{a}$ ($\mathbf{A}$ is $p\times(m+1)$ with rank $p$), the **F-statistic**

$$
F=\frac{(\mathbf{A}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}-\mathbf{a})^{\prime}(\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{X}^{\prime}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\prime})^{-1}(\mathbf{A}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}-\mathbf{a})}{p\cdot s^{2}}
$$

has an $F(p,\,n-m-1)$ distribution under $H_0$.

### The Gauss-Markov Theorem

Under (1) linearity $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}+\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, (2) strict exogeneity $\mathrm{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}|\mathbf{X})=\mathbf{0}$, (3) full rank of $\mathbf{X}$, and (4) $\mathrm{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\prime}|\mathbf{X})=\sigma^{2}\mathbf{I}_{n}$, the celebrated **Gauss-Markov theorem** states that the OLS estimator is the **best linear unbiased estimator** (BLUE): its covariance matrix is not larger than that of any other unbiased linear estimator. GLS inherits the same BLUE property when assumption (4) is relaxed to allow a positive definite error covariance.

---

## B.6 — Minimum Mean Squared Error (MMSE) Predictors

### MMSE Predictors of a Scalar Random Variable

We wish to predict a scalar $y$ using a vector of predictors $\mathbf{x}$ via $\hat{y}=g(\mathbf{x})$. The mean squared prediction error conditioned on $\mathbf{x}$ is

$$
\mathrm{MSE}_{\mathbf{x}}=\mathrm{E}_{y}[(y-g(\mathbf{x}))^{2}|\mathbf{x}].
$$

Differentiating with respect to $g(\mathbf{x})$ and setting to zero shows that the **Minimum Mean Squared Error (MMSE) predictor** is the conditional mean:

$$
g_{0}(\mathbf{x})=\mathrm{E}_{y}[y|\mathbf{x}].
$$

The conditional mean predictor minimizes the conditional MSE for every $\mathbf{x}$, and therefore also minimizes the unconditional MSE. Its prediction error is orthogonal to any function of $\mathbf{x}$:

$$
\mathrm{E}_{y,\mathbf{x}}[(y-g_{0}(\mathbf{x}))h(\mathbf{x})]=0 \quad \text{for any function } h.
$$

### Linear MMSE Predictors

When $f_{y|\mathbf{x}}$ is unknown or non-linear, we restrict to affine predictors $\hat{y}=\alpha+\mathbf{x}^{\prime}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and minimize $\mathrm{E}[(y-\alpha-\mathbf{x}^{\prime}\boldsymbol{\beta})^{2}]$. The **Linear MMSE (LMMSE)** predictor sets

$$
\boldsymbol{\beta}_{LMMSE}=\mathbf{C}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\sigma}, \qquad \alpha_{LMMSE}=\mathrm{E}[y]-\mathrm{E}[\mathbf{x}]^{\prime}\boldsymbol{\beta}_{LMMSE},
$$

where $\mathbf{C}=\mathrm{cov}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x})$ and $\boldsymbol{\sigma}=\mathrm{cov}(y,\mathbf{x})$. In the scalar case ($\mathbf{x}=x$):

$$
\hat{y}=\mathrm{E}[y]+\frac{\mathrm{cov}(x,y)}{\sigma_{x}^{2}}(x-\mathrm{E}[x]).
$$

The minimum MSE of the LMMSE predictor is

$$
\mathrm{MMSE}=\sigma_{y}^{2}-\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{\prime}\mathbf{C}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\sigma}=\sigma_{y}^{2}(1-R_{y,\mathbf{x}}^{2}),
$$

where $R_{y,\mathbf{x}}^{2}=\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{\prime}\mathbf{C}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\sigma}/\sigma_{y}^{2}$ is the fraction of variance of $y$ explained by $\mathbf{x}$.

### Conditional Distribution of a Multivariate Normal

Partition $\mathbf{x}=(\mathbf{x}_{1}^{\prime},\mathbf{x}_{2}^{\prime})^{\prime}$ with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}=(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1}^{\prime},\boldsymbol{\mu}_{2}^{\prime})^{\prime}$ and covariance

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}=\begin{pmatrix}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\\ \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}\end{pmatrix}.
$$

When $\mathbf{x}\sim\mathrm{MVN}(\boldsymbol{\mu},\boldsymbol{\Sigma})$, the distribution of $\mathbf{x}_{2}$ conditioned on $\mathbf{x}_{1}$ is multivariate normal with

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_{2|1}=\boldsymbol{\mu}_{2}+\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{1}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1}), \qquad \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{2|1}=\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}-\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}.
$$

The conditional mean formula $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{2|1}$ is also valid for all elliptical distributions with finite means (such as the $t$-distribution with 2 or more degrees of freedom).

---

## B.7 — Estimators via the Plug-In Principle

The **plug-in principle** provides a non-parametric estimator of a parameter $\theta$ by substituting the empirical distribution function $F_{n}$ for the true unknown $F$. The true parameter can be written as

$$
\theta(F)=\int g(r;\,F)\,dF(r)
$$

and the plug-in estimator is obtained by replacing $F$ with $F_{n}$ (the discrete distribution assigning probability $1/n$ to each of $r_{1},\ldots,r_{n}$):

$$
\hat{\theta}=\theta(F_{n})=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}g(r_{i};\,F_{n}).
$$

Applying this to the mean functional $g(r;F)=r$ gives the sample mean $\hat{\mu}=\bar{r}$. Applying it to the variance functional $g(r;F)=(r-\mu(F))^{2}$ gives the sample variance $\hat{\sigma}^{2}=\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(r_{t}-\bar{r})^{2}$.

---

## B.8 — Maximum Likelihood Estimators

### Location and Scale MLEs

Assume $N$ i.i.d. observations $\mathbf{r}=(r_{1},\ldots,r_{n})$ from a location-scale family. The **likelihood function** is

$$
L(\mu,s)=\prod_{i=1}^{N}\frac{1}{s}f\!\left(\frac{r_{i}-\mu}{s}\right),
$$

and the **log-likelihood** is

$$
l(\mu,s)=-N\ln(s)+\sum_{i=1}^{N}\ln f\!\left(\frac{r_{i}-\mu}{s}\right).
$$

With $s$ fixed, defining the **loss function** $\rho(x)=-\ln f(x)$, the MLE of $\mu$ is

$$
\hat{\mu}_{mle}=\underset{\mu}{\mathrm{argmin}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\rho\!\left(\frac{r_{i}-\mu}{s}\right),
$$

characterized by the **estimating equation**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N}\psi\!\left(\frac{r_{i}-\mu}{s}\right)=0, \qquad \psi(x)=\frac{d}{dx}\rho(x)=-\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)}.
$$

The function $\psi(x)$ is known as the **score function** in mathematical statistics.

**Normal Distribution MLE.** With $f(x)=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-x^{2}/2)$:

$$
\rho_{mle,normal}(x)=\ln(2\pi)/2+x^{2}/2, \qquad \psi_{mle,normal}(x)=x.
$$

The estimating equation gives $\hat{\mu}_{mle,normal}=\bar{r}$ (the sample mean), and $\hat{s}_{mle,normal}$ is the sample standard deviation from the biased variance estimator.

**Laplacian Distribution MLE.** With $f(x)=\tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-|x|)$:

$$
\rho_{mle,Laplacian}(x)=\mathrm{const}+|x|, \qquad \psi_{mle,Laplacian}(x)=\mathrm{sgn}(x).
$$

The location MLE is $\hat{\mu}_{mle,Laplacian}=\mathrm{MED}(\mathbf{r})$ (the sample median), and the scale MLE is the mean absolute deviation about the median.

**t-Distribution MLE.** With standard $t$ pdf of known degrees of freedom $\nu$:

$$
\rho_{mle,tdist}(x)=\mathrm{const}+\frac{\nu+1}{2}\log\!\left(1+x^{2}/\nu\right), \qquad \psi_{mle,tdist}(x)=\frac{\nu+1}{\nu}\frac{x}{1+x^{2}/\nu}.
$$

This is a non-convex optimization requiring numerical methods. Note that $\psi_{mle,tdist}(x)$ is bounded and non-monotonic, decreasing to zero as $|x|\to\infty$.

::: {#fig-mle layout-ncol=1}
![MLE loss functions ($\rho$) and score functions ($\psi$) for Normal, Laplacian, and t-distributions. For normal distributions, $\rho$ is quadratic and $\psi(x)=x$ is linear and unbounded. For Laplacian distributions, $\rho$ grows linearly and $\psi$ is bounded. For t-distributions, $\rho$ grows logarithmically and $\psi$ is bounded and non-monotonic — redescending to zero for large $|x|$.](1C__Users_tkpme_Dropbox_BookPCRA_Springer_Produ___B_Prob___Stat_for_PCRM_Plots_mleRhoPsiPlots.png){fig-alt="MLE rho and psi plots"}

MLE $\rho$ (loss) and $\psi$ (score) functions for location-scale estimation.
:::

The key observation: as tail fatness increases (normal $\to$ Laplacian $\to$ t), the $\rho$ function grows more slowly and $\psi$ transitions from unbounded to bounded. This motivates robust estimators that use bounded $\psi$ functions to limit the influence of outliers.

### Regression Model MLEs

For the cross-section regression model $r_{i}=\mathbf{b}_{i}^{\prime}\mathbf{f}+\varepsilon_{i}$ with i.i.d. scale-family errors, the log-likelihood is

$$
l(\mathbf{f},s)=-N\ln(s)+\sum_{i=1}^{N}\ln p_{0}\!\left(\frac{r_{i}-\mathbf{b}_{i}^{\prime}\mathbf{f}}{s}\right),
$$

and treating $s$ as known, the MLE is

$$
\hat{\mathbf{f}}_{mle}=\underset{\mathbf{f}}{\mathrm{argmin}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\rho\!\left(\frac{r_{i}-\mathbf{b}_{i}^{\prime}\mathbf{f}}{s}\right).
$$

- **Normal errors**: yields the Least Squares estimator.
- **Laplacian errors**: yields the Least Absolute Deviation (LAD/L1) estimator.
- **t-distribution errors**: yields a non-convex optimization with bounded $\psi$; a regression M-estimator is best viewed as an outlier-resistant generalization of these regression MLE equations.

For the normal distribution, $\hat{s}_{mle,normal}$ is the square root of the sample average of squared residuals; for Laplacian, $\hat{s}_{mle,Laplacian}$ is the sample average of absolute residuals.

### Covariance Matrix MLEs

The sample covariance matrix is the MLE when return vectors are multivariate normal. MLEs for non-normal multivariate elliptical distributions are discussed in the Cross-Sectional Factor Models chapter.

---

## B.9 — Useful Inequalities

**Markov's Inequality.** For any non-negative random variable $X$ and positive constant $a$:

$$
P(X\geqslant a)\leqslant\frac{\mathrm{E}[X]}{a}.
$$

**Moment Bound.** For non-negative $X$ and positive constants $a$ and $r$:

$$
P(X\geqslant a)\leqslant\frac{\mathrm{E}[X^{r}]}{a^{r}}, \qquad P(X\geqslant a)\leqslant\underset{r>0}{\inf}\frac{\mathrm{E}[X^{r}]}{a^{r}}.
$$

Philips and Nelson (1995) show the Moment Bound is tighter than Chernoff's Bound for positive tail probabilities.

**Chebyshev's Inequality.** For a random variable $X$ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$, and positive constant $b$:

$$
P(|X-\mu|\geqslant b)\leqslant\frac{\sigma^{2}}{b^{2}}.
$$

**Chernoff's Bound.** The tightest general-purpose inequality for random variables on the entire real line:

$$
P(X\geqslant a)\leqslant\underset{\theta>0}{\inf}\frac{\mathrm{E}(\exp(\theta X))}{\exp(\theta a)}, \qquad P(X\leqslant a)\leqslant\underset{\theta<0}{\inf}\frac{\mathrm{E}(\exp(\theta X))}{\exp(\theta a)}.
$$

**Jensen's Inequality.** For a convex function $g$ and random variable $X$:

$$
g(\mathrm{E}[X])\leqslant\mathrm{E}[g(X)].
$$

For strictly convex $g$ the inequality is strict; for concave $g$ the inequalities are reversed.

---

## B.10 — Limit Theorems for Random Variables and Distributions

### Convergence in Probability

An infinite sequence $X_{1},X_{2},\ldots$ **converges in probability** to $X$ if for every $\epsilon>0$:

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}P(|X_{n}-X|<\epsilon)=1, \qquad \text{written } X_{n}\overset{p}{\to}X.
$$

**Weak Law of Large Numbers.** If $X_{1},X_{2},\ldots$ are i.i.d. with finite mean $\mu$, then $\bar{X}_{n}\overset{p}{\to}\mu$.

### Convergence in Distribution

The sequence $X_{1},X_{2},\ldots$ **converges in distribution** to $X$ if $\lim_{n\to\infty}F_{n}(x)=F(x)$ for all continuity points $x$ of $F$, written $X_{n}\overset{d}{\to}X$.

### Slutsky's Theorem

If $X_{n}\overset{d}{\to}X$ and $Y_{n}\overset{p}{\to}c$ (a constant), then:

$$
X_{n}+Y_{n}\overset{d}{\to}X+c, \qquad X_{n}Y_{n}\overset{d}{\to}cX, \qquad X_{n}/Y_{n}\overset{d}{\to}X/c\ (c\neq0).
$$

### Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

Let $X_{1},X_{2},\ldots$ be i.i.d. with finite mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$. Then

$$
\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_{n}-\mu)\overset{d}{\to}N(0,\sigma^{2}), \qquad \text{equivalently}\quad \sqrt{n}\,\frac{\bar{X}-\mu}{\sigma}\overset{d}{\to}N(0,1).
$$

---

## B.11 — Estimator Asymptotic Results and Their Use

### Consistent Estimators

A **consistent** estimator $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ converges in probability to the true parameter value:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{n}\overset{p}{\to}\theta.
$$

Most estimators commonly used in practice are consistent: the sample mean for $\mu$, the sample variance estimators for $\sigma^{2}$, the sample standard deviation for $\sigma$, etc.

### Estimator Asymptotic Variance

For estimators whose variance converges to zero at rate $1/n$, the **asymptotic variance** $V(\theta;\hat{\theta}_{n})$ is defined via

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta)\overset{d}{\to}N(0,\,V(\theta;\hat{\theta}_{n})).
$$

**Example: Asymptotic Variance of the Sample Variance.** If i.i.d. returns $r_{i}$ have a finite fourth moment, then

$$
V(\hat{\sigma}_{n}^{2})=\mathrm{var}[(r_{1}-\mu)^{2}]=\mu_{4}-\sigma^{4}
$$

where $\mu_{4}=\mathrm{E}[(r-\mu)^{4}]$ is the fourth central moment.

*Proof sketch:* With mean zero (WLOG), $\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i}(r_{i}-\bar{r})^{2}=\tfrac{1}{n}\sum r_{i}^{2}-\bar{r}^{2}$, so $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\sigma}_{n}^{2}-\sigma^{2})=\sqrt{n}(\tfrac{1}{n}\sum r_{i}^{2}-\sigma^{2})-\sqrt{n}\bar{r}^{2}$. The first term converges to $N(0,a)$ by the CLT, and the second term equals $\bar{r}\cdot\sqrt{n}\bar{r}\to0$ by Slutsky's Theorem.

---

## B.12 — Delta Methods

### Univariate Delta Method

Given the asymptotic variance $V(\theta)=V(\theta;\hat{\theta}_{n})$ and a transformation $g$ with continuous first derivative $g^{\prime}$, the **univariate delta method** gives

$$
V(\theta;\,g(\hat{\theta}_{n}))=[g^{\prime}(\theta)]^{2}V(\theta).
$$

This follows from the Mean Value Theorem: $\sqrt{n}(g(\hat{\theta}_{n})-g(\theta))=g^{\prime}(\theta^{*})\cdot\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta)$ where $\theta^{*}$ lies between $\theta$ and $\hat{\theta}_{n}$; as $n\to\infty$, $\theta^{*}\overset{p}{\to}\theta$ and Slutsky's Theorem gives the result.

**Example: Volatility Estimator Asymptotic Variance.** Since $\sigma=\sqrt{\sigma^{2}}$, we have $g(t)=t^{1/2}$ and $[g^{\prime}(\sigma^{2})]^{2}=1/(4\sigma^{2})$, giving

$$
V(\sigma;\hat{\sigma}_{n})=\frac{\mu_{4}-\sigma^{4}}{4\sigma^{2}}.
$$

### The Bivariate Delta Method

For a scalar-valued transformation $g(\boldsymbol{\theta})=g(\theta_{1},\theta_{2})$ of a bivariate estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}=(\hat{\theta}_{1},\hat{\theta}_{2})^{\prime}$, the asymptotic variance of $g(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})$ is

$$
V_{\hat{g}}=\nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{\prime}\,\mathbf{V}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\,\nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta}),
$$

where $\mathbf{V}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ is the $2\times2$ asymptotic covariance matrix of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$.

**Example: Sharpe Ratio Asymptotic Variance.** The Sharpe ratio estimator is $\widehat{SR}=\hat{\mu}_{e}/\hat{\sigma}$, so $g(\boldsymbol{\theta})=\theta_{1}/\sqrt{\theta_{2}}$ with $\theta_{1}=\mu_{e}$, $\theta_{2}=\sigma^{2}$. Using

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}=\begin{pmatrix}\sigma^{2} & \mu_{3}\\ \mu_{3} & \mu_{4}-\sigma^{4}\end{pmatrix}, \qquad \nabla g(\boldsymbol{\theta})=\left(\frac{1}{\sigma},\ -\frac{\mu_{e}}{2\sigma^{3}}\right)^{\prime},
$$

the bivariate delta method gives

$$
V_{\widehat{SR}}=1-K_{3}\cdot SR+\frac{K_{4}+2}{4}\,SR^{2}
$$

where $K_{3}=\mu_{3}/\sigma^{3}$ is the coefficient of skewness and $K_{4}=\mu_{4}/\sigma^{4}-3$ is excess kurtosis. For normally distributed returns ($K_{3}=K_{4}=0$) this reduces to

$$
\mathrm{var}_{\infty}(SR_{n})=1+\tfrac{1}{2}SR^{2},
$$

the result of Lo (2002).

---

## B.13 — Estimator Standard Errors

For an estimator $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ with asymptotic variance $V(\theta)$, an approximate standard error is

$$
SE(\hat{\theta}_{n})\approx\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}V^{1/2}(\hat{\theta}_{n}),
$$

where the unknown $\theta$ is replaced by its estimate $\hat{\theta}_{n}$.

**Example: Volatility Estimator Standard Error.**

$$
SE(\hat{\sigma}_{vol,n})=\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\left(\frac{\hat{\mu}_{4,n}-\hat{\sigma}_{vol,n}^{4}}{4\hat{\sigma}_{vol,n}^{2}}\right)^{1/2}, \qquad \hat{\mu}_{4,n}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(r_{t}-\bar{r})^{4}.
$$

---

## B.14 — Fisher Information and MLE Asymptotic Optimality

Suppose $(r_{1},\ldots,r_{n})$ are i.i.d. with pdf $f(r;\theta)$. With $l(\theta;r)=\ln f(r;\theta)$, the **Fisher Information** for estimating $\theta$ is

$$
\mathcal{I}(\theta)=\mathrm{E}\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial l(\theta;r)}{\partial\theta}\right)^{2}\right].
$$

If $\hat{\theta}_{mle,n}$ is a sequence of consistent MLEs, then

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}_{mle,n}-\theta)\overset{d}{\to}N(0,\,\mathcal{I}^{-1}(\theta)).
$$

Furthermore, any other consistent estimator $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ satisfies the **information inequality** (Cramér-Rao lower bound):

$$
V(\hat{\theta}_{n};\theta)\geqslant\mathcal{I}^{-1}(\theta).
$$

The MLE achieves this bound asymptotically — it is asymptotically optimal among all consistent estimators.

---

## B.15 — Generalized Inverse of Distribution Functions

For a distribution function $F$ with "flat spots" (e.g., an empirical distribution function), the ordinary inverse does not exist. The **generalized inverse** (left-continuous quantile function) is defined as

$$
F^{\leftarrow}(p)=\inf\{r:\,p\leqslant F(r)\},\quad 0<p\leqslant1.
$$

### Application to Empirical Distribution Functions

For the EDF $F_{n}$ of a sample $r_{1},\ldots,r_{n}$ with no ties and ordered values $r_{(1)}<r_{(2)}<\cdots<r_{(n)}$:

$$
F_{n}^{\leftarrow}(p)=r_{(i)},\quad p\in\left((i-1)/n,\ i/n\right],\quad i=1,2,\ldots,n.
$$

The $p$-quantile of the EDF is $q_{F_{n}}(p)=r_{(\lceil np\rceil)}$ where $\lceil x\rceil$ is the ceiling function. For example, with $n=50$ and $p=0.01$: $q_{F_{50}}(0.01)=r_{(\lceil 0.5\rceil)}=r_{(1)}$.

For more careful treatments, one also considers the **right generalized inverse**

$$
F^{\rightarrow}(p)=\inf\{r:\,p<F(r)\},\quad 0<p\leqslant1,
$$

which is used by Acerbi and Tasche (2002) to define empirical VaR based on the right generalized inverse (with ceiling defined as the smallest integer strictly greater than $x$).
